data.title: Sandvik: Significant Overvaluation For 2026, I Remain Conservative
data.description:
Sandvik (SDVKY) is a company I've covered for years at this point. Most analysts for this business have a fairly consistent rating for the expectation for the business. This includes that it's unlikely to significantly outperform in the near term. This is reflected with a "HOLD" rating for the average SA analyst rating and also "HOLD" for the average Wall Street coverage, while current Quant coverage is at a "STRONG BUY".

In my last article, I gave the company a neutral "HOLD" rating. This rating has underperformed since that time. The company has gone up more than the market since, with details found in my last article, which you can find here.

I'll be providing a thesis for the company for the 2026E period and showing you why I don't necessarily believe in a significant upside for this company here. I could go on and say that the company is being forecasted at an average of lower PTs here by almost every analyst. A collective native S&P Global average of about 299.65 SEK, compared to the native SEK ticker of about 330 SEK, is another argument. But let's see if we can go deeper than this.

We have 3Q25 results, and we also have full-year results coming in about 2 weeks. That means that I will use this article as a bit of a preview as well for what I believe will happen.

If you follow my work on the company, you'll know that Sandvik is a market leader in highly specific commercial industrial equipment. Specifically, this is mining and metal-cutting. Its forward targets include something like a 7% overall annualized sales growth (top line) generated by inorganic growth. In this context, this means acquisitions. Because the company has so little leverage, there's little to prevent the company from doing exactly that - and the recent tech and current AI focus can, of course, enhance this further. Sandvik is also likely going to see further growth in its service revenue as well, given the installed base of its machines and products across the world - these are obviously less cyclical and have higher margins than standard machine sales.

Let's see what we have here.

Sandvik: Potential Upside in Mining Cut Short by Valuation, Pricing, and Risk

The company's performance in the previous periods has been solid. With a strong market demand for mining and engineering products driven by a general upcycle in the sector, what I'm seeing is that most of Sandvik's sales segments are up at the very least single digits, some double digits. The immediate segment risk comes from infrastructure and construction, which in Europe has been quite muted. The company has still been able to generate 7% top-line growth in order intake, which was part of the company's goal. However, total revenue decreased overall 4% over the past quarter.

EBITDA margins were stable at 19% (down only 40 bps), with savings contributing positively, but overall profit was actually down 200M SEK. FCF was down more than 1B SEK. This means that some of the cracks are at the very least starting to show here.

One thing I like about Sandvik is its focus on R&D and innovation. It doesn't just do this in systems and IT, but also in engineering and materials applications.

The company is currently in the midst of a significant transformation to become a more attractive and rounded business with less volatility. This has shown results. Better market positioning and better digital applications, among other things. The target for this program is towards the end of 2030, but results are showing now already. To show exactly what the company has been able to do, revenues of over 30B SEK per year have been divested already, while 22B SEK of revenues have been inorganically acquired.

The company has also increased its share of aftermarket products and services, which has gone from 31% in 2019 (of company revenues) to over 44% in 2024. Bulls for Sandvik could argue that this new mix justifies a higher overall multiple than the previous one. It's certainly an argument worth making, provided you're a Sandvik bull. Digital offerings as of 2024 consist of 5.1B SEK, and I forecast this in my earnings preview for 2025 to consist of at least 5.6B SEK. Sandvik has delivered on some of its growth and upside targets. But I argue that going forward, delivering on more of this upside will likely be harder than it was previously. This is because of inflated pricing and price levels we see on the market today.

My main issue with Sandvik is probably that it is a lot more complex than can be conveyed in a shorter article like this one. This is because of its significant variety of business segments, which in turn are exposed to a variety of end markets and trends. This variety of segments and exposures may at times suggest that the conglomerate, when compared to mining companies or engineering solutions, fails to capture the cyclicality and risk inherent in the business. A good reflection of this is the fact that this is far from the first time the company has done a restructuring to protect its profitability.

The best way to express what has been happening with Sandvik is illustratively or in graphics. I do this below.

This offers insight into the cyclicality and the potential ups and downs of the business. You should note, dear reader, that cyclicality and negative earnings are not just possible; they are recurring. You will also note that the company's current valuation is at a level where it has not been for at the very least 20 years. The closest was back in 2021, and that was still 35% less than the company is trading today, in terms of multiples to earnings. So the risk here is significant in the case of a potential drawdown.

Current estimates and forecasts for the company detail a significant upside. But this upside comes with a meaningful chance of negative forecast accuracy of over 50% on a 1-year basis, even with a 10% margin of error.

Sandvik has upside. The company's various operational segments have enough of a moat and enough of a unique upside to provide a barrier/safety against some of its peers. Aftermarket sales and service sales are proof of this. The company's market share in EV mining is nothing short of superb. At least in the EU, these provide the company with enough upside from a shift to green or sustainable mining. But some of the decisions, including the M&A of China-based companies, which are meant to increase Asian market shares, are, in my view, riskier than they first appear. They are especially riskier given the overall valuation trends.

I argue that these valuation trends provide a risk for the company to really drop. While it hasn't happened yet (and my rating has been "wrong", as such), I argue the higher the company goes, the higher this risk becomes. If you doubt it, you can simply look at the company's trading history.

Valuation for the Company - Upside is Almost Impossible to See Here, in My View

Seeing an upside at this price is near impossible. While secular growth prospects in mining have been responsible for much of the company's recent upside, I also say that these in no way make the company appealing. The last time the company was anywhere near appealing was probably back in 2024.

Since then, Sandvik has only become less and less attractive (while inversely providing higher and higher returns for those who hold the stock).

As of this article, I'll increase my PT to 205 SEK and my fair value to about 255 SEK. Why?

Because I believe in better EV growth prospects in mining and higher service and aftermarket sales. I now expect a full 3-4% revenue growth annually until 2028, or potentially 2029. This means it grows ahead of mining sector CapEx trends. I believe the EV shift will increase specialization in mining, and this should be reflected in the company share price and value - but it still means that the company is at least 35% overvalued and needs to become much cheaper before I'd consider investing any capital.

I don't think 20% EBITDA margins are possible for Sandvik, owing to increased costs even with its efficiency program. I argue the latest results reflect this. But I also believe this to be part of ongoing restructuring costs. I further say, as I've said before, that Sandvik is a highly cyclical and unsafe investment (in terms of volatility). This is due to the short-term nature of its equipment demand cycles. These are further related to swings in macro as well as commodity pricing.

Forecasting Sandvik shows us a potential annualized RoR of negative returns, even to the 20-year high premium of 17.3x. I forecast the fair value to be lower than that.

Even if you were to use somewhat of a more exuberant average, let's say you were to use about 20x P/E, this would still come only to about 4-5% per year, which to me is very underwhelming compared to what we can get next to this company.

Because of this, I would rate the company as a "HOLD" here and clearly call it a rotation target.

Risks & Considerations

In the case of a significant increase in service-related sales and income and improvement in its cost basis (it already outsources much of its production costs), Sandvik could see further premiumization. I believe, however, that many of these upsides have already been realized. Improving this further would require much more service revenue sales, well beyond what I believe to be possible from the business.

As of this update, I'd also argue that in terms of risk, the potential here is on the negative side. Everything from historical trends to cyclical end markets to swings in macro and commodities to restructuring programs and the costs for them is detailed, in my view, as asymmetric risk/reward. However, typically asymmetrical risk/reward is a positive, which it isn't for me in this case.

Because of this, I give the company an updated ADR SDVKY target of $22.5/share.

Thesis

Sandvik is a leading mining and equipment company out of Sweden with global operations and market-leading positions in key markets. It's A-rated, has a very solid dividend, and has good growth prospects. At least it does at the right valuation (if we're considering it as a solid investment). Even with the latest set of results that are somewhat negative in terms of profitability and growth but good in every other way, there is no end to this company's fundamental qualities that make it attractive for investment at the right price.

However, due to a decidedly overvalued price level, the company isn't all that attractive at this particular time. I'd consider it more appealing if around 17-18x normalized P/E, given the growth challenges the company seems likely to face on a forward basis.

My price target for Sandvik is now up to 205 SEK/share, and that's the high point given the risk that I see in the company's operations over the next few years. As of Q3'25, I fail to see the potential for significant improvement or premiumization and maintain my conservative "Hold" rating. In terms of an earnings preview, I see significant risk at this valuation for normalization in the company's share price, even in a positive turnout, and would therefore consider rotation prior to the company's results in about 1-2 weeks. This is especially true with recent tariff threats, which in this case would hit Sandvik directly.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which is not enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a valuation target of 205 SEK/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that is attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of January 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: FormFactor Q4 Preview: Riding The Memory Shortage Wave
data.description:
Introduction

FormFactor, Inc. (FORM) is due to report its Q4 and full-year 2025 results on the 4th of February, so I thought I’d go over what to expect and what I will be looking out for in terms of demand and operational performance. The company has seen its share price rise on the massive surge in demand for memory products, which will have FORM’s probe card sales; however, I think the share price appreciation already reflects this sentiment. Therefore, I will remain on the sidelines until we see a meaningful multiple contraction through improved profitability.

What To Expect

Analysts are expecting FORM to make $0.35 and $.022 in adjusted and GAAP terms, respectively, on around $210.3m in sales. That is around 11% y/y growth in sales, which is decent compared to the previous quarters that showed either declining, flat, or hardly increasing numbers. It seems that the company’s sales momentum is increasing, especially within the memory segment, which grew double-digits from growth in high bandwidth memory, or HBM.

The company’s guidance for the upcoming quarter is more or less in line with what the analysts are expecting, so there may not be a massive surprise, especially since over the last 10 quarters, sales and earnings were very close to what analysts expected.

However, I know that a lot has changed in the last quarter regarding the demand for memory in general, with prices skyrocketing across all end-markets due to the ever-growing demand for more power in AI and data center applications, so there is a chance that even the company’s guidance in October may be too low, and probably why its share price went up so much in such a short amount of time. So, there is a potential to beat expectations by a decent margin, given how the end-markets have developed since October.

What I'm Looking For

So, the first thing I am going to be looking out for is what kind of performance the company ended up having compared to what the management guided. I have a sneaky feeling that it will be at the top range of the guidance and may even be above the top range, which would signal to me that the demand for its most likely DRAM probe cards continues to be the main revenue driver.

The shortage in memory has pushed many of the companies involved in the space to amazing runs. Companies that I covered in the past and gave either a buy or a strong buy, for example, since my article back in September on Sandisk (SNDK), the share price 5x’ed, and only continues to rise. Same with Western Digital (WDC) and Micron (MU). So, any company that is involved in some way with the memory business and its shortage seems to be doing quite well. FORM’s stock price has already recovered well from its lows of $22 a share, and is currently up over 256% since then, and 83% in 1 year.

I don’t see a particularly massive boost in sales going forward compared to the actual memory players I mentioned, but the demand and shortages remain strong, so some boost is in the cards, in my opinion.

Due to this unprecedented demand, I will be looking out for comments regarding capacity. Back in Q3, FORM acquired a manufacturing facility in Farmers Branch, Texas, aimed to boost capacity for advanced packaging demand like HBM probe cards. I would like to get some more commentary on how this facility is going to be utilized going forward, and whether the opening target of late 2026 will be met or pushed either up or down the line. What kind of utilization rates should we expect across the firm’s facilities? I expect utilization rates to be close to full capacity, which should give the company better profitability across the board, leading to contraction in multiples.

We also know that the management was rather explicit when talking about the capex spikes in 2025, and that they are temporary, and will come down after the Farmers Branch buildout phase. However, I am going to look for any comments to see if that is still the case. Unprecedented demand for memory might push the company to spend even more capital on further capacity, which could mean looking for more facility space while the Farmers Branch site is being built up, so any word on capex forecasts changing, or if they still expect capex to normalize for 2026. I would say since most, if not all, hyperscalers and tech companies are increasing their capex spend considerably in 2026 over already massive spending in 2025, FORM will have no other choice but to follow the money, which means expanding further.

Is FormFactor A Buy Before Earnings?

A lot of positive sentiment regarding the company already pushed its shares up considerably in a short amount of time, which could mean that investors are already expecting a blowout quarter and guidance, so anything below these massive expectations will hit its valuation quite hard. Recently, some analysts increased their price targets to around $88 a share, some to $65 a share, so there is a lot of disconnect and uncertainty in place.

I think, given FormFactor, Inc.’s high multiples right now due to the lack of meaningful profitability and high share price, I’m going to wait a little longer. I want to see if FORM earnings improve considerably to the point where high multiples contract.

data.title: Mondelez: Solid Dividend, But Don't Get Your Hopes Up (Yet)
data.description:
The past six months have been one of the worst periods for Mondelez International, Inc. (NASDAQ:MDLZ) shareholders on record. The stock went from trading at levels of above $70 a share in July of last year to as low as $51 earlier this month, resulting in a drop of more than 25%.

On top of the sharp drop in recent months, MDLZ stock has also experienced significant volatility, which is a clear indication that investors were not anticipating such a hard hit on the company's bottom line from the recent increases of cocoa prices. One implication from this is that now there's a real possibility for the market sentiment to swing in the other direction after investors got caught off-guard.

As someone who has been optimistic about Mondelez long-term perspective, I did turn neutral on the stock in early July as it was trading at elevated multiples that did not properly account for risks ahead. Since then, MDLS is down by more than 15% and this opens up the question of whether or not the bottom is near?

To answer the question above, we should first take along at what's arguably the largest risk for Mondelez profitability in 2026 and the potential implications for profitability. Then we will take a closer look at how is MDLZ priced and if the current share price already fully accounts for recent increase of cocoa prices.

Pricing Power Vs. Higher Input Costs

After cocoa prices spiked in 2024 and now seem to have somewhat stabilized, Mondelez gross margins fell with a lag due to the company's hedging activities. The fiscal year 2025 would most likely be the year when the impact of the higher input costs fully manifests on Mondelez bottom line, and it seems that gross margin would stabilize around mid-20s.

Cocoa prices have also stabilized somewhat, although investors should keep in mind that forecasting future commodity prices is not worthwhile.

Nonetheless, Mondelez management recently expressed some optimism and 2026 futures are now significantly lower to the actual spot prices in 2024 and 2025 which could have a positive impact on margins.

But even if pressures of higher input costs ease in 2026, MDLZ would still need to rely on price increases in order to return to gross margins in the vicinity of 35% to 40%. And here lies the main risk for shareholders.

In response to the inflationary pressures from the 2022-23 period, MDLZ utilized its pricing power brought by the company's strong brand portfolio and the fact that the company operates in snacking product categories, which are characterized with high customer loyalty. As a result, the higher prices did not result in notable volume losses, and this had a positive impact on both margins and topline growth (see the green highlight on the graph below).

This time around, however, this does not appear to be the case. The past few quarterly reports show that the price increases are now met with a drop in the volume/mix for the company, which is a signal of customer exhaustion.

Based on that evidence, it appears highly likely that MDLZ management would take a measured approach and would abstain from significant price increases to the extent it did a few years ago. This scenario might be the better move from a long-term point of view as it will allow MDLZ to retain its high market share in key categories, such as biscuits, chocolate, pastries and snack bars.

It would also mean, however, that investors might need to get accustomed to a prolonged period of lower gross margins.

What that means is that MDLZ valuation multiples would most likely remain constant in 2026 as the stock is now trading in-line with its margins.

With that in mind, I do not anticipate MDLZ to outperform the market in 2026, unless we see a major change in the management's outlook during the upcoming Q4 report in early February. So far, the odds are stacked against MDLZ shareholders, but a better-than-expected dynamic between pricing and volume/mix alongside a more optimistic outlook for the year could provide a short-term tailwind for the stock.

The Role Of The Dividend

Even if prospects for MDLZ share price performance in 2026 appear bleak, a lot of investors could consider the stock for its dividend. The current yield of 3.4% is worth considering by long-term investors, as high-quality consumer staple businesses rarely trade at such levels.

At first glance, the dividend safety might appear to be at risk as free cash flow fell below the annual dividend layouts on a trailing-twelve month basis as of the end of Q3.

In addition to the lower profitability, working capital outflows also contributed to the lower free cash flow. More specifically, it was the increase in inventories which resulted in a cash outflow of almost $1bn for the first nine months of 2025. On top of that, accounts payable fell, which further aggravated the situation during 2025.

This working capital headwind, however, is unlikely to last beyond 2025 as inventories already reflect the higher commodity prices. This should allow MDLZ management to meet its $3bn free cash flow target for the year and cover the annual dividend outlay.

While the safety of the dividend is not an issue at the moment, the $3bn spent on share buybacks for the past 12-months period is likely to be reduced. Even though this is not a major issue for long-term shareholders, it would most likely put some more pressure on the share price in 2026 and this further solidifies my view that MDLZ is unlikely to outperform the market in the upcoming year.

Investor Takeaway

After a disastrous 6-month period for MDLZ shareholders, we are likely approaching the bottom for the share price. This, however, does not mean that MDLZ is not a Buy. Based on the dynamic between pricing and volumes, it appears likely that the quarterly gross margin will either remain at current levels or gradually creep up over the coming years. This would mean that shareholders are in for returns that are in-line with the sector at best.

data.title: 3M: Don't Keep Your 2026 Hopes Too High
data.description:
In early November, I upgraded 3M Company (MMM) from a Sell to a Hold rating but also expressed my view that caution was still needed as margin expansion was plateauing. When including today's (Jan 20th) post-earnings drop, the stock is down around 2-3% since the publication of my previous update, and so I believe my neutral stance has worked out fine. Today, we'll be taking an in-depth look at their 2025 Q4 earnings and the valuation to see whether my rating needs revising.

Below, it is shown that Q4 was a mixed quarter for 3M. Top-line growth weakened from already undesirable levels, and while margin expansion is always welcome, the rate of improvement continues to slow. Since their EPS growth has been quite dependent on operating margin strength, this may be a concern. In terms of their guidance, 2026 is shown to be another soft year overall, and in my view, that makes the stock's forward P/E discount to the Industrials sector fair. As a result, I have decided to maintain my hold rating on 3M for now.

H2 Improvement

In my previous article on 3M, I also discussed how the macro factors affected their business. While the environment remained unfavorable in the second half of 2025, the company was able to improve performance in most end markets. That's, of course, a good sign. As you can see above, consumer discretionary trends were softer than expected.

Personally, given that tariffs have eroded confidence in the U.S. and beyond, the weakness in consumer end markets really isn't that big of a surprise. For all other end markets, there was either net improvement or no change from H1 to H2, and so there are signs that their business is headed in the right direction. We'll, of course, go over their guidance later in this article, but judging from their performance in an undesirable macro environment, things are going alright.

Organic Growth Dips

In GAAP terms, 3M generated a total of $6.1 billion in sales in Q4, which represents a growth rate of 2.1%. This is a deceleration from Q3's 3.5%, and so business activity is clearly a bit sluggish right now. In adjusted terms, things weren't much better as the company posted adjusted sales of $6.0 billion. The 2.2% organic growth rate is slightly better than its GAAP counterpart, but there was also deceleration from the previous quarter as a sign that Q4 was soft. As shown by the business drivers stated on the right, results were mixed between their end markets.

Above, we discussed how the majority of their business saw improvement in H2 vs. H1. While that may be the case, there are also signs that their business activity has worsened since Q3, and so perhaps their current trajectory isn't as good as implied in the macro chart above.

Consumer Sentiment Weighs

In terms of the performance by segment, it should be quite clear that consumer confidence has been a headwind. In the charts above, you can see that while Q4 organic sales growth outperformed the full year for both Safety & Industrial and Transportation & Electronics, Consumer saw an organic sales decline of 2.2%, far worse than the full year's 0.3%. They explicitly state that there is market weakness and that the holiday season was slow.

Consumer weakness, particularly in the U.S., is primarily caused by relatively sticky inflation and the Trump Administration's tariffs. The Supreme Court may make a ruling on the legality of tariffs soon, but even if Trump is ruled against, many believe he'll find a workaround. Therefore, at this point, investors should expect 3M's consumer business to continue underperforming the other business groups moving forward.

Profitability is a Savior - for Now

Profitability was probably the highlight of 3M's Q4. As shown above, operating margin expanded by a respectable 140 basis points to 21.1% as overall efficiency and productivity improved. In the introduction, I mentioned that in my previous article, there were signs that margin expansion was slowing. That continued into this quarter. Back in Q3, there was a 170 basis points expansion in adjusted operating margin, and since the improvements have further cooled into Q4, there are indeed signs that profitability improvements are plateauing a bit.

You can also see above that 3M's EPS growth in the quarter was 9% as they reported $1.83 per share for the bottom line. Since this growth rate far outpaces the top line growth rate, you know that margin improvements have been key for 3M. However, with margin expansion continuing to slow, 3M will likely have to find ways to reaccelerate their business activity if they don't want EPS growth to continue slowing from here. Note that EPS growth was 10% back in Q3.

Capital Returns Suggest Caution

In their Q4, 3M generated a total of $1.3 billion in adjusted FCFs. That is unchanged from the year-ago quarter. If the company kept the capital return policy the same, then total dividends and share repurchases should also be unchanged YoY. For 3M, that was not the case. Back in 2024 Q4, the company returned a total of $1.1 billion to shareholders, but in 2025 Q4, they quietly decreased returns to just $0.9 billion. In my view, this potentially overlooked shift in policy shows that management remains very cautious regarding the near-term outlook. Speaking of the outlook, that's where we turn to next.

Full-year Outlook is Soft

For organic sales growth, 3M is expecting it to be at around 3% for the full year 2026. While that doesn't sound too exciting, it does potentially represent an acceleration from 2025's 2.1% adjusted organic sales growth rate. Therefore, there may be minor improvement in business activity moving forward. The good news might end there, however.

For operating margin, they are projecting just a 70 to 80 bps increase for 2026. In 2025, they had adjusted operating margin expansion of 200 bps, and so, like I stated earlier, there are continued signs that efficiency and productivity improvements are plateauing.

For the bottom line, they are expecting $8.60 at the midpoint, and that implies an annual growth rate of 6.7%. That would be a notable slowdown from 2025's 10% increase, and so the slightly better business activity will not be able to offset the weaker margin expansion. Lastly, note that their EPS projection missed consensus estimates, and so as a whole, I consider their 2026 full-year outlook to be soft.

Valuation Isn't That Attractive

As you can see above, when looking at the valuation multiples as a whole, 3M is actually not that cheap relative to the Industrials sector. Seeking Alpha's D rating for their valuation shows that current levels in the stock likely aren't attractive. In terms of my preferred measure, the non-GAAP forward P/E ratio, 3M does trade at a 7.25% discount to the sector's median. In my view, this shows that the valuation is around fair right now.

In today's article, it was shown that the fundamentals for 3M remain a bit shaky. Organic sales growth slowed with consumer sentiment weighing, and while margin expansion is a highlight, the rate of improvement is clearly slowing. In their 2026 guidance, it is shown that the next four quarters won't be overly strong either, both in terms of business activity and profitability, and so the overall outlook is soft and less than desirable. With that in mind, I view 3M's modest forward P/E discount as appropriate, but nonetheless, current levels in the stock likely don't offer a good entry point.

Concluding Remarks

In my view, 3M investors shouldn't keep their 2026 hopes for the stock too high. Q4 wasn't a terrible quarter, but at the same time, I believe it did little to show that the sluggishness will end. Full-year 2026 guidance also failed in that regard. Moving forward, as predicted, the plateauing of margin improvements will weigh on bottom line growth, and so the near-term outlook may have in fact dimmed. With that being the case, I view the valuation as being fair at best, and so I believe a reiteration of my hold rating is justified at this juncture.

data.title: Monolithic Power Systems Is A Silent Killer (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I’m picking up my coverage on Monolithic Power Systems, Inc. (MPWR) at a time when sentiment around AI has clearly become more fragile. The stock has benefited from AI exposure, but that cuts both ways. If investors continue to reassess AI positioning, MPWR is just as exposed to the downside as it was to the upside.

I don’t see the risk-reward as favorable at current levels, and would recommend investors to stay in the sidelines. I’m downgrading the stock to a Hold as I don’t see any near-term catalysts pushing the stock higher, especially given the shifting tone around AI. I remain bullish on the stock in the long run, but from where I’m standing, it has run out of fuel and will need to reset before it can offer a more attractive entry point.

Valuation has already priced in the AI angle, which leaves the company with little margin for error if demand normalizes. Without a clear catalyst to reaccelerate earnings expectations and with earnings visibility uncertain due to the company’s short lead time, upside appears limited. I think 2HFY26 will be a brighter spot for MPWR as the company has clearer visibility for demand, expecting “enterprise data growing in the range of 30% to 40% in 2026 for us,” according to the company’s CFO Bernie Blegen on Q3 '25 earnings call.

Past Coverage:

I last covered the stock in May of 2024, giving it a Buy as I saw that MPWR was one of the companies with real means to achieve AI-related revenue, making it “more resilient than the peer group to these industry waves of concern.” That has played out well, with the stock up over 42.5% since then, and earnings on the AI front gaining momentum in the quarters that followed.

Said peer group, which consists of analog companies such as On Semiconductor (ON), Texas Instruments (TXN), and Analog Devices (ADI), has performed poorly over the past 3 years, and even the past year.

As you can see below, MPWR was up over 73% over the past year, while ON was up 12.7%, TXN up 2.5%, and ADI up another 39%.

Over the past three years, the same pattern appears, with MPWR up 157%, ON down 8.4%, TXN up 9.2% and ADI up another 80%.

Before my May coverage, I had initiated MPWR with a Buy in late February of 2024. The stock is up over 45% since then, as I saw that

“MPS is an under-the-radar AI pick that hasn’t been overbought or overplayed yet; the stock hasn’t priced in its full AI upside…In my opinion, the company’s enterprise data segment is now its bread and butter, driving its outperformance and offsetting weakness from the industrial and auto-correction.”

That has played out well so far, but I don’t think the stock is as attractive as it was then. Slapping AI on anything comes with elevated expectations now, and “AI exposure” doesn’t cut it anymore.

Post Earnings Dip:

The company reported Q3 '25 in October, and the stock pulled back as expectations going into print were elevated. MPWR delivered a solid performance, with adjusted EPS of $4.73, above consensus of $4.63 and growing 16.6% year over year. Revenue also came in at $737.2 million, ahead of the $720.9 million consensus, up 18.9% year-over-year. Profitability was strong, with GAAP gross margin at 55.1%, in line with recent quarters. On the bottom line, net income rose to $178.3 million, up 23.4% year over year, while operating income increased to $195.2 million, reflecting 19.0% year-over-year growth and continued operating leverage.

So, what rubbed the market the wrong way? The enterprise data segment, which is now the company’s growth driver, is expected to be flat or down 20% as the company doesn’t have

“a lot of visibility into the first half of next year. We can definitely point to the normal drivers as far as both Enterprise Data and Automotive are very well positioned for new revenue ramps, but getting both the timing as well as getting that to balance out, we don't have a strong view on Q1 yet.”

Management guided rather conservatively for the current quarter, Q4 '25, and is expecting revenue up 1.8% to 5% sequentially, down from the 10.9% growth seen in Q3 '25. I think what really spooked investors is how little visibility management has to orders and margins. When asked about that on the call, Blegen said that

“What makes this cycle different than ones that we've experienced in the past is that the orders are more short term in nature. We're not seeing a large buildup in backlog in future quarters. And so without that visibility, it limits our capacity to be able to manage the mix of business that we want to be able to have expansion in gross margins. So for the foreseeable future, until the demand profile changes to elongate the buildup of backlog, I believe that we're going to be in sort of the steady range, plus or minus 20, 30 basis points in the mid-55%”

in gross margins.

Why I'm On The Sidelines: Investment Thesis

The analog landscape has been uneven since COVID. Large parts of the sector that remain heavily exposed to non-AI end markets, mainly automotive and industrial, have been stuck in a whirlwind of supply chain disruptions and demand volatility. That has weighed on most analog names and limited multiple expansion, and while MPWR has exposure in these markets, the story has played out differently.

Why? You guessed it. Differentiated exposure to AI and data center power. That AI angle is what allowed the stock to break away from the broader analog pack and materially outperform while peers tied to automotive and industrial markets struggled. The risk now is that, as sentiment around AI cools, the very factor that drove MPWR’s relative strength becomes a downside, especially as non-AI end markets remain in a weak recovery.

Valuation:

MPWR is expensive, overvalued even, and there is no question about that. On forward non-GAAP P/E, MPWR is at 80.6x, above TXN at 34.9x, 41.6x for ADI, but below ON at 134x. On EV/Sales, MPWR is at 17x and forward EV/EBITDA at 47x, both undeniably higher than peers, but they come with a stronger AI-related mix. ON trades at 4x EV/Sales and 15x EV/EBITDA because its earnings power is more exposed to automotive and industrial cycles. TXN and ADI sit in the middle, but neither offers the same combination of growth visibility and margin expansion. This is not a hold based on valuation alone, but the multiples don’t lie, and I expect some compression here in the near term.

From a technical perspective, the stock is starting to look stretched. MPWR closed around $1,033, comfortably above its EMA21 at $967 and EMA50 at $953, while the EMA200 near $855 remains the longer-term support. Near term resistance sits around $1,121 at the all-time high. RSI sits in the mid-60s, approaching overbought territory. Taken together, the setup supports consolidation or a modest pullback rather than immediate upside acceleration, especially after the recent recovery.

What's Next:

MPWR has built a reputation around differentiated, high-quality power solutions, and while it remains smaller than some of its analog peers, it has benefited from being an early mover in areas that now matter most. Its product portfolio is well positioned for enterprise data center demand, which is where AI-related server spending is most visible. That exposure is layered on top of a diversified end-market mix spanning consumer, automotive, computing, and storage, giving the company multiple revenue levers even as non-AI markets remain uneven. While competitive pressure is rising across analog, higher power requirements in AI servers work in MPWR’s favor, as they translate into greater power content per server.

The company is expected to report Q4 '25 in early February, and expectations, once again, are elevated. Though much of the downside is priced in, the stock recovered since that earnings dip, and is up around 3%. I think MPWR has bolstered its place in the AI supply chain, but I expect near-term volatility into print. The market expects revenue at $740.69 and EPS at $4.74.

With visibility on-demand limited, we could see upside surprise on print. But regardless, I’m not in the business of gambling, especially with a stock so tied to AI. Hence, MPWR is a Hold in my books.

data.title: RAPT Therapeutics: A Buy As GSK Acquisition Highlights Long-Acting IgE Candidate
data.description:
The last time I spoke about RAPT Therapeutics, Inc. (RAPT), it was in a Seeking Alpha article entitled "RAPT: CCR4 Inhibition Presses On For Now With Oncology Focus." With respect to this article, I primarily noted the development of its CCR4 antagonist zelnecirnon [RPT193] for the treatment of patients with asthma and atopic dermatitis [AD]. The drug was being evaluated to treat these patients in phase 2 clinical testing. The hope was that the FDA clinical hold would end up being lifted from this program.

Unfortunately, this was not the case, and the company chose to completely close down this entire program. The main thing is that it would focus on its next-generation CCR4 antagonists in preclinical development. Along with another CCR4 antagonist in its pipeline known as tivumecirnon, which is in phase 2 testing with KEYTRUDA [pembrolizumab] for the treatment of patients with gastric cancer.

It has partnered this asset with Hamni for the territories of Korea, Taiwan, and China [including Hong Kong and Macau]. It is seeking other partners to further advance this outside of these territories. Despite not delivering on its CCR4 antagonist pipeline, I'm happy to state that the company had made significant progress with an anti-immunoglobulin E [IGE] monoclonal antibody, though. In particular, it has already initiated the phase 2b prestIgE trial using ozureprubart [formerly RPT904] for the treatment of patients with IgE-mediated food allergy. The initiation of this particular phase 2b prestIgE trial was also on the basis that this potential best-in-class anti-IgE monoclonal antibody [mAb] performed well in treating patients with chronic spontaneous urticaria [CSU].

The promise of ozureprubart is that it targets the same epitope of XOLAIR [omalizumab] from Novartis (NVS) and subsidiary of Roche (RHHBY), Genentech. It has been approved for IgE-mediated food allergy, asthma, Chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps [CRSwNP], and CSU. The point is that ozurebrubart is a bio-better anti-IGE mAb because it has an extended half-life. I had a Buy rating since then, and I'm maintaining this same rating. Not only because of all this progress achieved, but also because there was another exciting piece of news released. Today, it was announced that GSK plc, aka GlaxoSmithKline (GSK), would pay $2.2 billion to acquire RAPT Therapeutics to add ozureprubart as part of its respiratory, immunology, and inflammatory development pipeline.

With all of this said, the stock has traded higher by 126.81% since I last wrote about it. The beauty of the food allergy drug development program of this drug is that it can be applied to other IgE-mediated immune disorders. The key advantage being ozureprubart being a long-acting IgE with an extended half-life, allowing the dosing of up to once every 12 weeks.

Food Allergy Program Established POC Competitor, and Phase 2b Study Already Underway

As I noted above, GlaxoSmithKline entered into an agreement to acquire RAPT Therapeutics for $2.2 billion based on the prophylactic food allergy drug ozureprubart. Again, the beauty of this drug is that it is versatile in that it could be applied to treat many other IgE-mediated immune disorders.

What I want to go over in this section are two key items. One being the fact that RAPT already initiated the phase 2b prestIgE trial. I'm going to go over the design of this study for sure, but just know that there are two reasons why the company chose to initiate this study. First, there was the release of positive data from RAPT and its China partner Shanghai Jeyou Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. [Jeyou] using ozureprubart to treat patients with chronic spontaneous urticaria [CSU] from a phase 2 study. Secondly, XOLAIR [omalizumab] has already established the ability to reduce allergic reactions to multiple food allergens, allowing it to have a broad label. I will be going over both of these items below, but before moving forward, it is important to highlight what food allergens are and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being.

A food allergy is a specific immune response by the body to a given type of food that a person consumes. In a mild case, it can be having symptoms such as hives and itching. However, in more severe cases, there is an issue of something occurring known as anaphylaxis [swelling of the throat and trouble in being able to breathe effectively]. A food allergy is not to be confused with food intolerance. That is, you just can't handle eating such food, and it does not involve an immune system reaction. For those who are intolerant to some foods, some symptoms could be bloating of the stomach and/or gas. The prevalence in the United States is huge, which is, in my opinion, the first reason why GlaxoSmithKline acquired RAPT. Consider that there are roughly 17 million people in the United States dosed with food allergy [RAPT Therapeutics Corporate Presentation November 2025, slide #12], including at least 3.5 million children.

The thing is that XOLAIR [omalizumab] is the standard of care [SOC] for CSU. Plus, it has been approved for other IgE-mediated immune disorders like food allergy. If omalizumab is already in the marketplace doing so well, how can a drug from RAPT like ozureprubart overcome it? The basis of this is that ozureprubart is a bio-better antibody. What does this even mean? In essence, it has been designed to be long-acting, and that is thanks to its extended half-life design. Right now, patients with SOC therapies have to be treated once every 2 weeks [Q2W] or once every 4 weeks [Q4W].

What ozureprubart can offer is the ability to have patients only need to receive therapy once every 8 weeks [Q8W] and/or possibly once every 12 weeks [Q12W]. With no need to increase dosing in CSU, plus achieving several positive patient compliance measures [RAPT Therapeutics Corporate Presentation November 2025, slide #10], the company is well-equipped to produce a potential best-in-class anti-IgE mAb.

The first item to go over is proof of concept established by the IgE mechanism of action [MOA] by XOLAIR, which is in terms of food allergy. It was shown in a study that the drug allowed patients to take a higher threshold dose across multiple food allergens. The best-case scenario shown is the peanut allergen patients, where 67% consumed the threshold dose versus only 7% of placebo patients [RAPT Therapeutics Corporate Presentation November 2025, slide #7]. The point is that since RAPT's ozureprubart targets the same epitope as omalizumab, plus ozureprubart having an extended half-life, it should perform better than its counterpart. The second item to note is proof of concept from RAPT and its partner in China, Jeyou. This stems from a phase 2 trial of ozureprubart [RPT904 [JYB1904]] as a monotherapy to treat patients with chronic spontaneous urticaria (CSU).

In the trial, it was noted that these CSU patients receiving this drug Q8W or Q12W ended up having comparable safety/efficacy to that of omalizumab given Q4W. To this end, its partner Jeyou is set to advance this program into phase 3 clinical testing in China. The main point here is that further POC in using an anti-IgE mAb to treat IgE-mediated immune disorders is highly plausible. The mechanism of action [MOA] has been established, and all that remains now is the execution of late-stage trial testing.

Another item to note, which was bullish, is that RAPT had already initiated the randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled phase 2b prestIgE study for the program using ozureprubart to treat patients with IgE-mediated food allergy. This trial is expected to recruit up to a total of 100 patients who are to be randomized 2:2:1 to the following regimens as follows:

  • 40 patients taking ozureprubart [RPT904] once every 8 weeks [Q8W].
  • 40 patients taking ozureprubart once every 12 weeks [Q12W].
  • 20 patients were given placebo comparator.

This is to entail part 1 of this entire study, which is to last for 24 weeks. Why is that? That's because the 2nd 24-week period after the first one [first 24-week portion] is slated to be the extension and placebo-crossover period. Patients on placebo cross over to be given the drug and evaluated. The primary endpoint is set to establish the ability for a patient to consume food without dose-limiting symptoms as part of a food challenge. This is only to be evaluated for part 1 or the first 24-week period of this phase 2b study, where the drug is compared to that of a placebo. The primary endpoint is broken down depending on food allergens as follows:

  • Proportion of patients who consume a single dose of ≥600 mg of either peanut or cashew protein without dose-limiting symptoms.
  • or a single dose of ≥1000 mg dealing with consumption of milk, egg, or walnut protein without dose-limiting symptoms.

With everything I have described above, plus the nature of ozurprubart being a long-acting potential best-in-class IgE antibody, likely led to the culmination of GlaxoSmithKline wanting to acquire it. Especially for the food allergies program. The population of IgE-mediated targeting is there, as 94% of food allergy is caused by this type of mediated antibody reaction.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, RAPT Therapeutics had cash and cash equivalents and marketable securities of $157.3 million as of September 30, 2025. This cash on hand was further bolstered by the fact that it had enacted an underwritten public offering of common stock. That is, it sold 8,333,334 shares of its common stock at $30 per share to raise approximately $250 million before expenses. The truth is that this company didn't even need to be acquired at all.

With the total cash on hand, it believed it had enough to fund its operations into mid-2028. This was enough not only to fund its company but was already set to also be in a position to initiate phase 3 studies for ozureprubart to treat patients with CSU as well. Still, if it is acquired now by GlaxoSmithKline, it doesn't have to worry about needing to raise cash in the future.

Risks to Business

There are several risks to consider when looking into this buyout of RAPT Therapeutics by GlaxoSmithKline. The first risk to consider would be the initiation of the phase 2b prestIgE study, using ozureprubart to treat IgE-mediated food allergy. Even though its partner Jeyou established positive data from a phase 2 study in being able to treat patients with CSU, there is no assurance that this will translate to positive data for RAPT's own food allergy prestIgE study. The same can be said with another drug that has been tested. Omalizumab given to patients once every 4 weeks showed broad coverage of being able to treat multiple food allergens, as I went over above.

The risk here is that there is no assurance that, upon the release of the phase 2b prestIgE study data, it will be positive. Nor that it will match closely in terms of safety and/or efficacy to that of omalizumab in food allergen testing.

The second risk to consider would be that of the phase 2 study of its partner Jeyou using ozureprubart to treat patients with CSU. The risk here is that this study, showing that this drug given Q8W and Q12W has comparable efficacy to omalizumab, was done in China. There is no assurance that the expected phase 3 study in China is going to produce a similar outcome to that shown from the released phase 2 China study. Also, if the phase 3 trial somehow happens to fail, then it will be a huge negative for GlaxoSmithKline, which is paying $2.2 billion to acquire RAPT Therapeutics.

The third and final risk to consider would be as part of RAPT Therapeutics' preclinical pipeline. The reason why is because it is in the process of evaluating next-generation CCR4 antagonists to treat patients with cancer and other disorders. The risk here is that the company already failed to do well with the first-generation CCR4 antagonist, zelnecirnon. Although this program wasn't stopped on the basis of lack of efficacy, the safety issues it faced had caused an FDA clinical hold. Thus, a big risk with the preclinical next-generation CCR4 antagonists is that there is no guarantee that they won't have similar safety issues and/or eventually suffer from a clinical hold yet again.

Conclusion

There is no doubt that RAPT Therapeutics suffered from a major setback in dealing with its first-generation CCR4 antagonist drug, zelnecirnon, for the treatment of patients with asthma and AD. However, the good news is that it had a pipeline of other drugs to fall back on. In particular, it has done extremely well with ozureprubart in targeting several IgE-mediated disorders. In particular, it initiated the phase 2b prestIgE study using ozureprubart to treat patients with IgE-mediated food allergy. The most promising aspect of this drug, which is the main reason why I believe GlaxoSmithKline chose to acquire RAPT Therapeutics, would be because of the extended half-life it has.

This allows for more convenient dosing of Q8W and Q12W, compared to XOLAIR, which is dosed Q4W. With the company having already initiated its phase 2b prestIgE study using ozureprubart to treat IgE-mediated food allergy, plus the acquisition of the company by GlaxoSmithKline, I believe that it is important to maintain a Buy rating on RAPT's stock.

For GlaxoSmithKline, it is facing patent expiration for its HIV drug dolutegravir in 2028. The hope was that it would acquire a more late-stage asset to counter this. Plus, it needs to prove that acquiring late-stage assets that have extended half-life technologies can be monetized over other currently available therapies. With all that being said, I believe that it is important to initiate a Hold rating on GlaxoSmithKline's stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Equity Residential: Markets Underestimate Its Geographic Advantages
data.description:
Reflecting increasing pessimism about the trajectory of rental inflation, Equity Residential (EQR) shares have been a poor performer over the past year, losing over 11% of their value. This underperformance has come even as EQR’s own performance has been reasonable thanks to its focus on lower-supplied, legacy markets. Given its own fundamentals, I upgraded shares to a “Buy” in August, but this call has been premature with EQR falling 4% since then, weighed down by broader macro concerns. With updated financials and a revised macro picture, now is a good time to revisit Equity Residential.

Apartment Rents Have Disappointed

The primary headwind for shares is clearly concern about rental inflation. As you can see below, rents are down 1.3% from a year prior. Rents, of course, surged post-COVID and then decelerated as supply came online and stimulus eased. Over 2024 and into early 2025, rental inflation gradually rebounded and appeared poised to move back into positive territory; however, over the past six months, it has turned back lower to run at the weakest level since COVID.

The reason for my optimism on rental inflation was focused on supply dynamics. Given surging rents and low borrowing costs, construction boomed post-COVID, and as this new supply has hit the market, rents have weakened. We have seen construction slow meaningfully, which will help to alleviate this pressure. It can take ~18 months for projects to be completed, so supply lags new construction dynamics by 1–2 years. I expect new supply to continue declining at least for another 9 months, and by H2 2026, supply should be at normal levels. Indeed, in its markets, EQR expects supply to decline by 35% in 2026.

The fact that rents have reversed lower even as the supply headwind steadily fades is noteworthy and has surprised me. This points to the weakening being on the demand side. To an extent, the Administration’s more aggressive immigration enforcement is likely playing a role in reducing the number of households and thereby housing demand. Beyond that, years of elevated inflation have sapped consumers’ excess savings and made it harder for landlords to pass on inflation. With overall inflation softening, some of this pressure may be fading, though I expect the rental inflation to be muted in 2026, running at about 0% nationally.

EQR's Focus Insulates It From Some Pressure

Now, it is important to note that there is meaningful geographic dispersion, which largely aligns with how much new supply has been added over the past five years. The Sun Belt, once the leader, is now the clear laggard. Meanwhile, the NYC-area, Boston, and San Francisco still have rising rents as they have not been inundated with as much new construction. The Washington D.C. market has weakened of late, which likely is due to cutbacks in the federal workforce. These are markets where EQR’s business is largely focused, which has helped to insulate its own results to an extent. I expect these markets to continue outperforming the Sun Belt through 2026 given relative supply dynamics.

As you can see below, EQR’s business is primarily focused in these legacy markets. The Northeast, DC, and West Coast are nearly 90% of its business with just limited exposure across several expansion markets. Notably, EQR has no material Florida exposure, and market dynamics there appear to be the weakest, especially in the Tampa and Orlando markets. This exposure profile is a clear near-term benefit. While national rents may be flattish in 2026, rents in its geographies should be up closer to ~2% in my view.

EQR Results Have Been Solid

In fact, while there has been significant new supply across the country, we are seeing EQR withstand this well given its geographic exposure and focus on higher-quality properties. In fact, its occupancy is running above last year’s level and is strong at 96.3%. That gives it about half the vacancy as the national average. Thanks to this strong physical occupancy, when the company last reported financials on Oct. 28, Equity Residential beat estimates by a penny, earning $1.02. EQR’s average tenant has a $175k annual income and is spending just 20% of their income on rent. These factors should make their demand relatively durable in the face of macro uncertainty.

Despite lower rents nationally, same-store revenues jumped by 3% in Q3, though with expenses up 3.6%, net operating income was up a more modest 2.8%. Lease rates were up 2% with new leases down 1% and renewal rates up 4.5%. During periods of market softness, renewals typically outperform new leases. Given the cost of moving, if tenants are happy with their apartment, they are likely to stay, even if they could save a few percent moving elsewhere. I expect this divergence to persist in 2026.

Importantly, EQR maintains an excellent balance sheet with just 4.4x debt/EBITDA. I am comfortable with apartment REITs carrying 5-5.5x debt/EBITDA. This provides about $1 billion of incremental debt capacity, giving EQR the flexibility to buy properties if there are opportunities to add assets cheaply from distressed sellers. Aside from this, it can be active in returning capital to shareholders. We are seeing this; in the third quarter, EQR repurchased $100 million of stock. Aside from these opportunistic buybacks, shares offer a 4.5% dividend yield, which I view as highly secure.

Alongside results, EQR tightened guidance to $3.98-$4.02 from $3.97-$4.03, with same-store sales rising 2.5%-3%. Back in August, I was targeting $3.95-$4.01 in 2025 FFO, which drove my $70 price target. I expect full-year 2025 results to come in at about $4.00, which is actually towards the high end of my own expectations.

EQR Is A "Buy"

In other words, the weakness we have seen in EQR shares is not due to a deterioration in near-term results. Rather, 2025 results are in line with my expectations. Instead, we are seeing multiple compressions as investors price in a more muted go-forward rental inflation trajectory. While the apartment rental market has indisputably softened, this softening is more pronounced in the highly supplied expansion markets where EQR has very little exposure. To an extent, I believe EQR shares have been caught up in a macro fear that is not fully applicable to shares.

Indeed, I expect EQR to grow FFO by 2%-3% in 2026 to $4.08-4.12, given modest rental inflation, a slightly lower share count, and completed development projects. I still view a ~17x FFO multiple as appropriate, given its significantly under-levered balance sheet and long-term growth of ~4% (2.5%-3% inflation and 1%-1.5% apartment count growth). As such, I reiterate my $70 price target. I view EQR as one of the more compelling options in the apartment REIT space, given its geographic positioning. I expect shares to rally towards $70 this year as investors come to appreciate the Sun Belt pressures weighing on national rent indices are not applicable to EQR’s business, enabling a recovery in shares. EQR is still a "Buy."

data.title: Nvidia: GPUs Don't Matter Anymore (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis Summary

At CES, Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang warned us about what comes next in AI and how this changes the market.

GPUs are no longer the unit of competition in AI.

The winners of tomorrow will be the companies that can develop a holistic system that is optimized for cost-effective performance.

This means efficient GPUs, yes, but also improvements in software, networking, and deployment.

For investors, this distinction is critical.

The next phase of Nvidia’s growth will be driven less by advancements in hardware and more by improvements in software and systems.

This is where Nvidia must maintain an edge if it wants to dominate the market.

Nvidia’s Strategic Shift

Nvidia became a market leader by being the only name in town when it came to AI GPUs.

Now, as other companies begin to catch up in terms of hardware, Nvidia is already focusing on what comes next

We mentioned that we build chips. But as you know, Nvidia builds entire systems now. And AI is a full stack.

Source: Jensen Huang, CES.

From the insights shared at CES, I believe there are three key points that will shape the AI market going forward.

  • AI systems, rather than components

  • Cost per unit of intelligence rather than raw power

  • Integration with the next layer, physical AI.

Nvidia is positioning exactly according to these three principles.

The GPU is Just the Base Layer Now

This is the uncomfortable truth that many investors miss. Even inside NVIDIA’s ecosystem, the GPU itself is increasingly interchangeable.

Value is now created at the system level, and what customers increasingly buy from Nvidia is not a GPU but a tightly integrated system.

This is why Nvidia now often refers to “AI factories,” rather than just GPU sales, and in an AI factory, each component matters.

NVLink and other high-bandwidth interconnects determine how effectively compute scales across racks.

Networking fabrics such as Spectrum-X control latency, congestion, and data flow as models grow larger and more distributed.

On top of this hardware stack sit Nvidia’s software layers, from CUDA to inference optimisation, which translate raw compute into usable intelligence.

Platforms like Omniverse then close the loop, enabling simulation, testing, and deployment in virtual environments before models ever touch the real world.

In this architecture, the GPU is essential, but it is no longer where the majority of value is created.

In isolation, a GPU is just compute, and investors need to realise this.

From Generative AI to Physical AI

Perhaps the most underappreciated message from CES was Nvidia’s reframing of the next AI wave.

AI is now grounded in the physical world, through Nvidia’s technologies for training, inference and edge computing.

These systems can be trained on synthetic data in virtual worlds long before interacting with the real world.

Source: Nvidia Blog, CES Presentation.

The last cycle was about generating text, images, and code.

The next cycle is about AI that understands and operates in the physical world and can be applied to robots, AVs, factories, and real physical networks.

Training these systems requires:

  • Massive simulation

  • Synthetic data generation

  • Vision-language-action reasoning models.

And this is precisely where Nvidia’s Vera Rubin platform is designed to excel.

By co-designing GPUs, CPUs (Vera), interconnects (NVLink 6), DPUs, memory hierarchies, and simulation software into a unified system, Vera Rubin enables persistent workloads while optimizing for power, cooling, and cost efficiency.

The platform treats each rack as a single intelligent node, allowing training and inference at scale that is extremely difficult for competitors to replicate.

In this architecture, GPUs are still essential, but the Vera Rubin stack turns them into one component of a holistic intelligence system.

Extreme codesign — designing all these components together — is essential because scaling AI to gigascale requires tightly integrated innovation across chips, trays, racks, networking, storage and software to eliminate bottlenecks and dramatically reduce the costs of training and inference, Huang explained.

Source: Nvidia Blog, CES Presentation.

This is Very Bullish for Nvidia

Of course, GPUs still matter, but the true battlefield has now moved beyond the hardware, and this is good for Nvidia.

Competitors have been catching up in terms of GPU technology.

Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) is now making similarly powerful GPUs, and Alphabet’s (GOOGL) tensor processing units, or TPUs, have also been a big success.

Indeed, the value of GPUs may soon begin to decrease, and the money will be made elsewhere.

What these components can’t actually do is replicate Nvidia’s end-to-end platform.

Once a customer designs their AI infrastructure, software stack, and workflows around Nvidia systems, the GPU choice becomes secondary.

The real moat is the lock-in created by the whole ecosystem Nvidia has created, which can’t be replaced.

And this is, in fact, amplified as we move into physical AI, which adds yet another layer of complexity.

Risks

Having said this, there are some risks we can't overlook.

Selling systems rather than components increases complexity and raises customer expectations.

There is also the risk of regulatory scrutiny as Nvidia’s influence over AI infrastructure deepens.

And, of course, the valuation is dependent on continued growth.

If AI spending slows, Nvidia’s premium pricing could come under pressure.

Final Thoughts

GPUs are necessary, but they are no longer the most important part.

The real product is applied intelligence, delivered cheaply, reliably, and at scale. This, in my view, makes the company’s moat wider and more durable than ever.

Nvidia continues to be a high-conviction long-term hold with a Strong Buy rating in my portfolio.

data.title: Strategy: No Longer Worth It
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The darling of digital treasuries, Strategy has lost the shine it once had. Although Bitcoin has been capturing institutional attention, collapsing premiums raise the question: Is holding Strategy worth it?

Shareholders are entitled to the net asset value (NAV) of Strategy in the event of liquidation. In 2025, Strategy's mNAV (market cap-to-NAV) gained a notable premium, peaking at a multiple of 2.4x in 2025. At that time, buying a dollar's worth of the stock would have given you exposure to $0.40 of Bitcoin holdings. Why pay for $0.40 of something when you can just buy one for one?

This isn't the case anymore, with Strategy now trading at an mNAV of 0.94; buying a dollar of stock gets you $1.06 in Bitcoin exposure. If an investor has reason to believe premiums will expand, this may be an attractive opportunity. In my view, the hype has died down. Although mNAV may fluctuate above and below 1x during bullish and bearish crypto periods, the days of 2x multiples are behind us.

If multiples don't re-expand to higher levels, the risk/reward starts to become unattractive. After all, buying Bitcoin is 1-to-1, and that comes without the added risk of preferred dividends and debt. That risk has grown in recent months, with Strategy adding a cash reserve to address this. However, this takes away from the proceeds-to-Bitcoin exchange and hurts existing investors more than usual. With that said, only investors with a strong conviction that Bitcoin will rise further should purchase this stock.

Given the current multiple near 1x mNAV, I'm giving this stock a rating of Sell. It's unlikely multiples will re-expand to former levels, and dilution concerns have persisted. This makes this digital asset treasury (DAT) more aligned with Bitcoin's price movements, and the risk-reward is an unattractive option when compared to the underlying asset.

Collapsing Premiums

Last summer, DATs ran wild, with sky-high premiums and announcements generating billions in valuation. For example, Bitmine Immersion Technologies (BMNR), the popular Ethereum treasury, saw its stock rise by over 3,000% in less than a week after announcing a $250 million private placement. This was common during the time; however, announcements today don't get the same treatment.

This hype has now died down, taking mNAV along with it. This was helped by a fall in crypto prices but was likely to occur regardless. Unless crypto prices are on a constant rise, premiums eventually fall toward 1x NAV. This disrupts equity raises, a key part of the DAT flywheel, making each raise essentially a 1-to-1 exchange from cash to Bitcoin when proceeds are used to buy Bitcoin. This continues the close alignment of treasury and Bitcoin price movements.

Previously, a high mNAV premium allowed DATs to issue shares much higher than their NAV. This benefited existing shareholders, who were able to increase their Bitcoin exposure by simply holding. This was presented to investors as the "Bitcoin Yield," which was a measure of the increase in Bitcoin per share. In short, current holders rely on new investors to increase their Bitcoin-per-share via share issuance above NAV.

After establishing a $1.44 billion cash reserve to fund dividend obligations, the yield went negative for the first time in Strategy's history, causing its removal from the company's dashboard.

Indices

Index inclusion has been one of Strategy's highest priorities, which allows it access to thousands of passive funds that are then required to hold it. That’s the biggest benefit, but visibility, liquidity, and cost of capital are all improved as well.

Recently, Strategy faced a scare on this front, with MSCI proposing a rule to exclude companies where crypto holdings exceed 50% of their total assets. If this passed, Strategy would have as much as $2.8 billion in outflows. MSCI recently announced it would continue allowing its inclusion. However, this may be temporary, with MSCI stating they would launch a "broader consultation" on how these companies should be treated.

The Strategy was also expected to be added to the S&P 500 in September, a move that would have given it billions more in passive inflows. They ended up not being selected, a move that has been widely debated. This was despite its market cap peaking around $100 billion, making it larger than 80% of companies in the S&P. A likely factor is its role as a type of "Bitcoin ETF," which would then influence the U.S. stock market's greatest benchmark.

They were included in the Nasdaq-100, unlocking inflows from indexes like QQQ, which have over $250 billion in assets. This comes from their classification as a technology firm, where a small portion of revenue comes from its software analytics. However, reclassification could come in March, when Nasdaq does its benchmark review. This is a possible scenario, considering the vast majority of Strategy's operations revolve around Bitcoin and would label it a financial company, something ineligible for Nasdaq inclusion.

Dilution

Dilution is a part of Strategy's business model, as they look to acquire more Bitcoin. As mentioned earlier, the impact on shareholders depends on whether they are existing or new and where the mNAV stands.

With falling premiums, cash raises are increasingly difficult, requiring more share issuance than prior raises. This has driven shares outstanding to increase by around 60% since last year.

Traditionally, all proceeds would go to Bitcoin purchases; however, recent declines in crypto prices have caused Strategy to build a cash reserve. As of late December, the reserve is now over $2 billion. This provides room for Strategy to meet debt and dividend obligations but also dilutes the "Bitcoin Yield" they attempt to achieve and ruins the 1-to-1 exchange of proceeds to Bitcoin. This further reaffirms the reasoning to go out and buy Bitcoin yourself, instead of being involved in other obligations.

Debt and Preferred Shares

Strategy has found that common shares aren't the only way to raise money, as debt and preferred shares have also funded Bitcoin purchases.

Preferred shares have been a strong source of funding for the company, with the potential to raise significantly more capital. This aligns with their 42/42 plan, which targets total proceeds of $84 billion through offerings. This comes in the form of four types of shares: STRK, STRF, STRD, and STRC. Although they differ in seniority, all provide a dividend, something Strategy has adjusted for with their cash reserve. With the current numbers, Strategy's dividend runway is just under 3 years, which eases near-term concerns. Note, this runway does have the ability to shorten if more preferred equity is issued.

Debt comes in the form of convertible senior notes spread out across years until 2032. This adds up to $8.2 billion in notes due, something that will eventually have to be taken into consideration. These notes have virtually no interest, with holders having the option to be repaid in full or convert into common stock. Either option poses challenges for Strategy and should be a key risk for long-term holders, with the first maturity date in mid-2028.

Risks

If Bitcoin faces extreme price changes, this could relieve or increase pressure on Strategy, which could not only affect asset valuation but could also hurt or support the ability to raise capital and fund the flywheel.

If multiples return to a premium, Strategy will have the ability to raise capital more easily and improve flywheel operation. On the other hand, continued dilution and falling crypto prices could cause further multiple compression.

Outlook

Overall, with a falling premium and rising dividend obligations, Strategy is more exposed than ever. Even if crypto does continue to have strong gains, it's unlikely Strategy will enter a period similar to early 2025, when multiples were expanding, and capital was easy to come by. The honeymoon period is over, and Strategy will have to navigate its toughest market yet to return to its former glory.

With the added pressures, I'm giving this stock a Sell. It's unlikely Strategy will outperform Bitcoin, given the measures taken to fund prior purchases. Even with a rising Bitcoin price, Strategy will face dilution to fund dividend repayments and eventually, bond repayments. All of these factors take away from the appeal Strategy once had and again raise the question: why not just buy Bitcoin?

data.title: Fifth Third: Q4 Calms Fears, But Valuation Is Full
data.description:
Shares of Fifth Third Bancorp (FITB) have been a solid performer over the past year, gaining over 13%. Shares have also recovered solidly from tumult in the late summer/early fall as the company disclosed losses releases to problems in the private credit market. These losses drove me to downgrade shares to a Sell in September, though this call was a bit aggressive. Shares have gained 6% since then, consistent with the market’s 5% gain, suggesting my prior Hold rating was still most appropriate. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit FITB. I remain cautious.

In the company’s fourth quarter, Fifth Third earned $1.08, which beat estimates by $0.07 as revenue grew 7% to $2.3 billion. Balance sheet trends were consistent with prior quarters, including a combination of slow growth and improving margins.

That said, investors can breathe a sigh of relief as credit losses returned to normal following a weak Q3. Capital is solid, and its pending M&A is set to close shortly, though I am cautious on the ultimate benefits of the deal.

Balance Sheet Growth Is Slow

Deposits were up 1% from last year to $168.4 billion. While deposits meet my threshold of “stable,” growth has been slow, particularly given its strong Sun Belt presence. Growth has been weighed down by a 40bps cut in deposit costs, as FITB strongly passed on Fed rate cuts to customers. I was encouraged to see noninterest-bearing balances rise for a third straight quarter. They are up $1.7 billion from last year to $41.8 billion. After a prolonged period of pressure, it appears safe to say that balances here have bottomed, which will be supportive of margins going forward.

On the other side of its balance sheet, FITB’s loan balances were flat sequentially and have essentially hovered just over $123 billion all of 2025. Loan demand has been muted given macro uncertainty and elevated borrowing costs. With somewhat lessened tariff uncertainty and lower borrowing costs, I do expect improvement in 2026, but loan growth is likely to still lag economic growth. This does reduce the need to gather deposits, and we will likely see further efforts to cut deposit costs as a result.

Within this portfolio, FITB has $17.5 billion of commercial real estate (“CRE”) exposure. Credit quality here remains strong with just 19bps of nonperforming loans. This portfolio is aided by the fact that half of loans are owner-occupied, making them a quasi-corporate exposure. Just 5% of the portfolio is non-owner occupied office. I am comfortable with FITB’s CRE exposure, and at ~14% of its total portfolio, FITB has less CRE exposure than most regional banks. FITB has a larger consumer portfolio, at 40% of loans, but I expect to see some credit improvement as delayed benefits from the 2025 tax bill in H1 2026 help to relieve affordability strains.

FITB has a $50 billion securities portfolio. With a 4.1 year average duration, it will take time for low-yielding securities to fully roll off and be invested at current market yields. This process will be gradual but will benefit net interest income. The bank’s net interest margin (“NIM”) was 3.13%. This was flat sequentially but up 16bps from a year earlier. The gradual roll-off of low yielding securities has helped to boost NIM, and this tailwind should continue in 2026, albeit at a slower pace.

Noninterest income was up 11% from last year at $711 million. Wealth and asset management led the way, up 13% to $185 million. Rising markets have mechanistically lifted AUM, which in turn increases advisory fees. With fees typically charged in arrears, we will see a sequential rise in Q1 from the Q4 market rally. With equities remaining firm thus far in 2026, that bodes well for ongoing revenue growth from this segment this year.

Credit Quality Stabilized

Net charge-offs were 40bps in Q4. As you can see below, there was a pop in Q4, given high-profile, large private credit losses. This prompted a renewed examination of its loan exposure, and FITB is confident these losses are not systemic of broader underwriting issues. It is encouraging to see charge-offs to return to normalized levels, adding to the hope that Q3 losses were truly one-time in nature.
