Non-performing assets remain healthy at 65bps, just slightly above the long-term norm. As you can see, NPLs are down about $56 million over the past year. That said, more loans are in the early stage of delinquencies with $360 million 30–89 days past due, up from $303 million last year. I do expect some sequential improvement in Q1, given a favorable tax refund season, but this bears watching.

Against this, Fifth Third has set aside $2.4 billion for credit losses, equal to 1.96% of loans. This coverage is down 12bps over the past year, primarily due to improvement on consumer loans. FITB maintains a very strong reserve position, enabling it weather unexpected losses. Provisions are more than 3x NPLs, which exceeds my 2.5x target. This excess reserve position provides FITB with significant loss-absorbing capacity and provides downside protection against potential deterioration in the economic backdrop. I expect ACL/loan coverage to decline modestly in 2026, given my view that a recession is unlikely.

Capital Is Sufficient, But Not Excellent

Fifth Third has a 10.8% common equity tier 1 (CET1) capital ratio, which is up 20bps from last year. Capital excludes a $2.8 billion loss sitting in accumulated other comprehensive income (AOCI). Just given the forward curve and pull to par as bonds mature, this loss should improve by $500 million in 2026. As you can see below, CET1 including AOCI is up 100bps over the past year to 9.1%. While not a regulatory calculation, this is helpful in considering the true economic position of the bank. I would like to see FITB operate in the 9.5-10.0% range, and I expect it to achieve this during late 2026.

Thanks to buybacks, its share count is down 2% from last year, though FITB did not do repurchases in Q4 due to its pending CMA deal and Q3 net charge-offs. Shares also offer a 3.2% dividend yield. I view this payout as highly secure. After the integration of CMA, we could see buybacks resume with more meaningful repurchases possible in the second half of the year.

Comerica Deal Is A Strategic Question Mark

Fifth Third has also received approval to acquire Comerica (CMA) and expects the transaction to close on February 1st. While there should be ample opportunity for synergies given its overlapping geographic presence, I am cautious on the merits of this deal. I have been negative on CMA’s standalone financials for some time, given the loss of low-cost deposits, which will weaken its margins. Ironically, CMA was slated to lose these low-cost deposits (via the Treasury Direct Express program) to FITB, so these will all stay in-house, negating a prior net win for FITB. While I am supportive of FITB’s efforts to add scale to better compete against the national banks, I view this deal’s long-term merits skeptically.

Alongside results, FITB provided initial guidance for 2026, which includes 11 months of Comerica. FITB expects essentially no loan growth. I expect strategic trimming of CMA loans to offset organic growth. That said, we should see some margin benefits from the maturities of fixed rate assets. Most importantly, FITB expects net charge-offs to tick down slightly. This speaks to added confidence that 2025 loan losses are in the past and should give investors greater confidence about underlying loan quality.

I Would Avoid FITB Given M&A And Valuation

Back in September, I was targeting $3.70-$4.00 of standalone 2026 EPS. I now see $3.90-$4.25 in 2026 EPS, recognizing M&A can create some one-time in results. This leaves shares right around 12x earnings, towards the higher end of regional banks. For a bank that still needs to build pro forma capital and is undertaking a large deal that I am skeptical of, I struggle to view this as a compelling opportunity, especially as potential NIM pressure inside legacy CMA offsets NIM benefits inside legacy FITB. I would rather invest elsewhere in the sector, like KeyCorp (KEY) or Huntington (HBAN), given cleaner stories.

While shares are not overly expensive, I see limited upside and see shares underperforming the sector. On principle, I also struggle to recommend holding shares of a company engaged in large-scale M&A that I am skeptical of. As such, I remain a Sell on FITB. At a full valuation, even with confirming that credit quality is fine, potential overhangs from Comerica leave me cautious, and I would avoid FITB, looking elsewhere in the sector for better opportunities.
